title: Revisiting the high-field phase diagram of the topological cubic helimagnet SrFeO$_{3}$
authors: Masaki Gen, Shun Okumura, Shusaku Imajo, Taro Nakajima, Karel Prokes, Shunsuke Kitou, Yusuke Tokunaga, Yoichi Nii, Koichi Kindo, Yoshimitsu Kohama, Shintaro Ishiwata, Taka-hisa Arima
abstract: The cubic perovskite SrFeO$_{3}$ is a prototypical centrosymmetric itinerant magnet that hosts a quadruple-${\mathbf Q}$ hedgehog-antihedgehog lattice and exhibits a complex magnetic-field-temperature phase diagram. Yet, the microscopic mechanism underlying the emergence of its versatile multiple-${\mathbf Q}$ phases remains unresolved. Here, we elucidate the field-orientation dependence of the magnetic phase diagram and establish an effective spin Hamiltonian for SrFeO$_{3}$ that incorporates a cubic single-ion anisotropy together with bilinear and biquadratic interactions in momentum space. In addition, we observe magnetoelastic signatures of a first-order valence transition upon entering the forced FM phase at 40 T, which would be attributed to the suppression of negative charge transfer. These findings emphasize the pivotal importance of electronic itinerancy arising from the formation of a ligand-hole band in stabilizing multiple-${\mathbf Q}$ phases.
summary: This paper revisits the complex magnetic phase diagram of the topological helimagnet SrFeO3 under high magnetic fields. It discovers a new intermediate phase and demonstrates that the transition to the forced ferromagnetic state involves a valence transition related to ligand-hole density redistribution. These findings highlight the critical role of electronic itinerancy in stabilizing complex multiple-Q magnetic structures.
main_problem: Resolving the microscopic mechanism of multiple-Q phases in the topological cubic helimagnet SrFeO3 and understanding the field-orientation dependence of its magnetic phase diagram.
main_result: The study identifies a new intermediate-field phase (Phase VI) and provides evidence for a valence transition driven by the redistribution of ligand-hole density when entering the forced ferromagnetic phase.
method: The researchers combined pulsed high-field magnetization, magnetostriction, and ultrasound measurements with single-crystal neutron scattering and Monte Carlo simulations using an effective spin Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is SrFeO3, a cubic perovskite itinerant magnet characterized by a ligand-hole 3d5L electronic configuration. The theoretical framework uses an effective bilinear-biquadratic spin Hamiltonian with cubic single-ion anisotropy.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), differential susceptibility (dM/dH), longitudinal and transverse magnetostriction, longitudinal sound velocity, and magnetic Bragg peak intensities (Q1-Q4).
important_parameters: Magnetic field strengths up to 50 T, temperatures down to 4.2 K, field orientations H || [001] and H || [111], and the modulation vector q approx 0.13 r.l.u.
main_assumptions: The effective spin model assumes that the essential magnetic features can be captured by bilinear and biquadratic interactions and cubic anisotropy, while the valence transition is treated as a subtle redistribution of ligand-hole density.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure; Figure 3 displays the metamagnetic transition, negative volume jump, and elastic anomalies; Supplemental figures show the evolution of neutron scattering profiles and integrated intensities of magnetic reflections.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system and problem, details the experimental and computational methods, presents the reconstructed high-field phase diagram and new phase discovery, discusses the role of anisotropy and valence transitions, and concludes with a summary of the microscopic mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of SrFeO3, llustrated using the VESTA software [71]. (b) Schematic electronic conﬁgurations of the 3d4 state (left) and the ligand-hole 3d5L state (right). (c) H–T phase diagram for H ∥[111] (red solid line) and H ∥[001] (blue dashed line). The deﬁnitions of phases I–V follow Ref. [38], whereas phase VI is newly discovered in the present study. “PM” denotes the paramagnetic phase. The phase boundaries drawn with thick lines indicate possible valence transitions from the 3d5L to the 3d4 state, as suggested by the anomalous magnetoelastic responses observed in this study. Only data for the ﬁeld-increasing process are shown. The top bars indicate the corresponding magnetic phase diagrams, where “F” denotes the forced FM (or paramagnetic) phase.

FIG. 3. Magnetic-ﬁeld dependence of magnetization (a), longitudinal (left) and transverse (right) magnetostriction (b), and the longitudinal sound velocity for k ∥u ∥[111] (c) measured at various temperatures for H ∥[111]. For clarity, the curves are vertically shifted except for T = 4.2 K. In panels (a) and (b), data for the ﬁeld-increasing (decreasing) process are shown in dark (light) colors. Only data for the ﬁeld-decreasing process are shown in panel (c). The inset of panel (a) shows an enlarged view of the M–H curve at 4.2 K near the saturation ﬁeld.
